```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
dnf install -y aide

# Create a backup of the current AIDE configuration
cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak

# Modify the AIDE configuration to send emails on change detection
sed -i 's/^#email_alert/email_alert/' /etc/aide.conf
echo "mailto = root" >> /etc/aide.conf

# Ensure the AIDE cron job exists and is configured correctly
cat <<EOF > /etc/cron.daily/aide_check
#!/bin/bash
aide --check | mail -s "AIDE Integrity Check Results" root
EOF

chmod +x /etc/cron.daily/aide_check

# Ensure the AIDE cron job is enabled
chkconfig --add aide_check
chkconfig aide_check on
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide > /dev/null; then
    dnf install -y aide
fi

# Backup existing AIDE configuration
cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak

# Function to add extended attribute checks to AIDE configuration
add_xattr_to_aide() {
    local config_file="/etc/aide.conf"
    local new_config=$(cat <<EOF
$(grep -v '^#' $config_file)
# Add extended attribute checks
*.* xattrs
EOF
)

    echo "$new_config" > /tmp/aide.conf.tmp
    mv /tmp/aide.conf.tmp $config_file
}

# Apply the changes
add_xattr_to_aide

# Rebuild the database
aide --init
aide --check
```

```bash
    # Enable FIPS mode
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the system-wide cryptographic policy is set to the default-strength profile
if ! grep -q "CRYPTO_POLICY=default" /etc/crypto-policies/config; then
    echo "CRYPTO_POLICY=default" | sudo tee -a /etc/crypto-policies/config > /dev/null
fi

# Apply the new cryptographic policy
sudo update-crypto-policies --set default

# Verify the current cryptographic policy setting
current_policy=$(sudo update-crypto-policies --show)
if [[ "$current_policy" != "default" ]]; then
    echo "Failed to set the cryptographic policy to default."
    exit 1
fi

echo "Cryptographic policy set to default successfully."
exit 0

```bash
    echo "CRYPTO_POLICY=default" | sudo tee -a /etc/crypto-policies/config > /dev/null
```

# Ensure GnuTLS uses the system's cryptographic policy
if ! grep -q "crypto_policy" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "crypto_policy = system;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

# Disable insecure protocols in GnuTLS
if ! grep -q "min_protocol_version" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "min_protocol_version = TLS1.2;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

if ! grep -q "max_protocol_version" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "max_protocol_version = TLS1.3;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

# Restart services using GnuTLS to apply changes
services=$(ps aux | grep gnutls | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq)
for service in $services; do
    if systemctl is-active --quiet $service; then
        sudo systemctl restart $service
    fi
done

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure GnuTLS uses the system's cryptographic policy
if ! grep -q "crypto_policy" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "crypto_policy = system;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

# Disable insecure protocols in GnuTLS
if ! grep -q "min_protocol_version" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "min_protocol_version = TLS1.2;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

if ! grep -q "max_protocol_version" /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf; then
    echo "max_protocol_version = TLS1.3;" | sudo tee -a /etc/gnutls/gnutls.conf > /dev/null
fi

# Restart services using GnuTLS to apply changes
services=$(ps aux | grep gnutls | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq)
for service in $services; do
    if systemctl is-active --quiet $service; then
        sudo systemctl restart $service
    fi
done
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the script is run as root

    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1
fi

# Set the keymap to ignore Ctrl-Alt-Del
gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.plugins.power power-button-action 'ignore'

This script sets the `power-button-action` setting to `ignore` using `gsettings`, which prevents the system from rebooting when the Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence is pressed. It then locks this setting using `dconf lock`, ensuring that it cannot be changed by individual users. The script checks if it is being run as root to avoid permission issues.

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure gpgcheck is set to 1 in all repository configuration files
find /etc/yum.repos.d/ -type f -exec sed -i 's/gpgcheck=0/gpgcheck=1/' {} \;

# Ensure gpgkey is specified in all repository configuration files
gpgkeys="https://example.com/RPM-GPG-KEY-example"
for repo_file in /etc/yum.repos.d/*.repo; do
    if ! grep -q "^\s*gpgkey=" "$repo_file"; then
        echo "gpgkey=$gpgkeys" >> "$repo_file"
    fi
done

# Update yum cache to apply changes
yum clean all

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the pam_tally2 module is used for account locking
if ! grep -q "pam_tally2.so" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "auth required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail deny=5 unlock_time=900" >> /etc/pam.d/system-auth
fi

# Ensure the pam_tally2 module is used for account unlocking
if ! grep -q "pam_tally2.so" /etc/pam.d/password-auth; then
    echo "password required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail deny=5 unlock_time=900" >> /etc/pam.d/password-auth
fi

# Ensure the maxloginfail parameter is set in /etc/security/login.defs
if [ "$(grep -E '^MAXLOGINFAIL' /etc/security/login.defs | awk '{print $2}')" != "5" ]; then
    sed -i 's/^MAXLOGINFAIL.*/MAXLOGINFAIL 5/' /etc/security/login.defs
fi

# Ensure the faillock command is used to reset failed login counters
if ! type faillock &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y util-linux
fi

# Ensure the faillock service is enabled and running
systemctl enable faillock
systemctl start faillock
```

This script ensures that the `pam_tally2` module is configured in both `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` and `/etc/pam.d/password-auth` to lock accounts after 5 failed login attempts and unlock them after 900 seconds. It also sets the `MAXLOGINFAIL` parameter in `/etc/security/login.defs` to 5. Additionally, it installs the `util-linux` package if the `faillock` command is not available and enables and starts the `faillock` service to manage failed login attempts.

if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1

echo "kernel.panic_on_oops = 0" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
sysctl -p > /dev/null

# Create a systemd service to ensure the setting persists across reboots
cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/disable-cad.service
[Unit]
Description=Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c 'echo kernel.panic_on_oops = 0 > /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-cad.conf'
ExecReload=/bin/sh -c 'echo kernel.panic_on_oops = 0 > /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-cad.conf && sysctl -p'

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable disable-cad.service
systemctl start disable-cad.service

#!/bin/bash

# Function to update password expiration for a user
update_password_expiration() {
    local username=$1
    chage -M 365 "$username"
}

# Get a list of all local users
local_users=$(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $1 != "nobody" {print $1}' /etc/passwd)

# Iterate over each local user and update their password expiration
for user in $local_users; do
    current_max_age=$(chage -l "$user" | grep 'Maximum number of days between password change' | awk '{print $5}')
    if [ "$current_max_age" -ne 365 ]; then
        update_password_expiration "$user"
    fi
done

```bash
    chage -M 365 "$username"
}

# Get a list of all local users
local_users=$(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $1 != "nobody" {print $1}' /etc/passwd)

# Iterate over each local user and update their password expiration
for user in $local_users; do
    current_max_age=$(chage -l "$user" | grep 'Maximum number of days between password change' | awk '{print $5}')
    if [ "$current_max_age" -ne 365 ]; then
        update_password_expiration "$user"
    fi
done
```

#!/bin/bash

# Function to check if a user is interactive
is_interactive_user() {
    local username=$1
    getent passwd "$username" | cut -d: -f7 | grep -q '/bin/bash$'
}

# Iterate over all local users
for user in $(getent passwd | awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $3 < 65534 {print $1}'); do
    # Check if the user has an interactive shell
    if is_interactive_user "$user"; then
        # Change group ownership of all files and directories in the user's home directory
        find "/home/$user" -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -exec chgrp --preserve-root {} {} \;
    fi
done

```bash
#!/bin/bash
```

# Define the target permissions in octal
TARGET_PERMS=0740

# Function to check and set permissions for a file
set_permissions() {
    local file=$1
    local current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
    
    if [ "$current_perms" -gt "$TARGET_PERMS" ]; then
        chmod $TARGET_PERMS "$file"
        echo "Set permissions of $file to $TARGET_PERMS"
    fi
}

# Iterate over all human users with home directories
for user in $(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $6 != "" {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do
    home_dir="/home/$user"
    set_permissions "$home_dir/.bashrc"
    set_permissions "$home_dir/.bash_profile"
    set_permissions "$home_dir/.profile"
done

# Set permissions for root's initialization files
root_home="/root"
set_permissions "$root_home/.bashrc"
set_permissions "$root_home/.bash_profile"
set_permissions "$root_home/.profile"

echo "Remediation completed."

```bash
#!/bin/bash
```

# Ensure the necessary package is installed
dnf install -y rsyslog-gnutls

# Configure rsyslog to use TLS for forwarding
sed -i 's/^module(load="omfwd")$/module(load="omfwd" template="RSYSLOG_SyslogProtocol23Format")/' /etc/rsyslog.conf

# Add the following lines to enable TLS and specify the remote server details
cat <<EOF >> /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging.conf
$template RemoteLogs,"<%protocol-version%>1 %timestamp:::date-rfc3339% %HOSTNAME% %syslogtag%%msg%\n"
*.* @@remote-server:514;RemoteLogs
EOF

# Enable and start the rsyslog service
systemctl enable rsyslog
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Ensure the SELinux policy allows TLS connections
setsebool -P rsync_full_access on
setsebool -P rsync_anon_write off

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the necessary packages are installed
dnf install -y rsyslog-gnutls

# Configure rsyslog to use TLS for forwarding
cat <<EOF > /etc/rsyslog.d/30-tls.conf
module(load="omfwd")
action(type="omfwd"
       protocol="tcp"
       target="remote_collector_host"
       port="514"
       template="RSYSLOG_SyslogProtocol23Format"
       tls="on"
       tls.caFile="/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt"
       tls.certFile="/etc/pki/tls/certs/rsyslog-cert.pem"
       tls.keyFile="/etc/pki/tls/private/rsyslog-key.pem"
)
EOF

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Ensure the configuration file has the correct permissions
chmod 640 /etc/rsyslog.d/30-tls.conf
chown root:root /etc/rsyslog.d/30-tls.conf
```

This script installs the `rsyslog-gnutls` package, which provides support for TLS encryption in rsyslog. It then configures rsyslog to forward logs over TLS to a specified remote collector host. The TLS settings include paths to the CA certificate, server certificate, and private key files. After applying the configuration, it restarts the rsyslog service to ensure the changes take effect. Finally, it sets the appropriate permissions on the configuration file to restrict access.

sed -i '/^action(type="omfwd")/a\
StreamDriverMode="1"\n\
StreamDriverAuthMode="x509/name"\n\
StreamDriverPermittedPeers="remote-collector-hostname"' /etc/rsyslog.conf

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Ensure the change is persistent across reboots
chkconfig rsyslog on

# Ensure the sysctl configuration file exists

# Set the necessary sysctl parameters to disable IPv6 ICMP redirects
echo "net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/90-ipv6.conf > /dev/null
echo "net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/90-ipv6.conf > /dev/null
echo "net.ipv6.conf.lo.accept_redirects = 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/90-ipv6.conf > /dev/null

# Apply the changes immediately
sudo sysctl --system > /dev/null

# Verify the settings have been applied correctly
if [ "$(sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects)" == "net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0" ] && \
   [ "$(sysctl net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects)" == "net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0" ] && \
   [ "$(sysctl net.ipv6.conf.lo.accept_redirects)" == "net.ipv6.conf.lo.accept_redirects = 0" ]; then
    echo "IPv6 ICMP redirects disabled successfully."
else
    echo "Failed to disable IPv6 ICMP redirects."
fi

# Function to disable IPv4 ICMP redirects
disable_icmp_redirects() {
    local interface=$1
    local sysctl_file="/etc/sysctl.d/90-ipv4-icmp.conf"

    # Check if the sysctl file exists, if not create it
    if [ ! -f "$sysctl_file" ]; then
        touch "$sysctl_file"
    fi

    # Add or update the sysctl settings to disable ICMP redirects
    echo "net.ipv4.conf.$interface.send_redirects = 0" >> "$sysctl_file"
    echo "net.ipv4.conf.$interface.accept_redirects = 0" >> "$sysctl_file"
    echo "net.ipv4.conf.$interface.secure_redirects = 0" >> "$sysctl_file"
    echo "net.ipv4.conf.$interface.shared_media = 0" >> "$sysctl_file"

    # Apply the changes immediately
    sysctl --system
}

if [ -n "$default_interface" ]; then

#!/bin/bash

# Function to check if a mount point is a non-root local filesystem
is_non_root_local_filesystem() {
    local mount_point=$1
    # Check if the mount point is not the root filesystem and is a local filesystem
    [[ "$mount_point" != "/" ]] && findmnt -n -o TYPE "$mount_point" | grep -q 'ext[2-4]\|xfs\|btrfs'
}

# Function to apply the necessary protections
apply_protections() {
    local mount_point=$1
    # Create a directory for device nodes if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "$mount_point/dev"
    # Mount --bind /dev to the mount point's dev directory
    mount --bind /dev "$mount_point/dev"
    # Set appropriate permissions
    chmod 755 "$mount_point/dev"
}

# Iterate over all mounts and apply protections where needed
for mount_point in $(findmnt -t ext2,ext3,ext4,xfs,btrfs -o TARGET); do
    if is_non_root_local_filesystem "$mount_point"; then
        apply_protections "$mount_point"
    fi
done

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots
echo "Ensuring persistence across reboots..."
cat <<EOF >> /etc/fstab
# Bind mount /dev to prevent device node interpretation on non-root local filesystems
$(awk '$2!="/"&&$2~/^\/[^ ]*$/&&!/tmpfs/ {print $2}' /proc/mounts) /dev none bind 0 0
EOF

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Function to check if a mount point is a non-root local filesystem
is_non_root_local_filesystem() {
    local mount_point=$1
    # Check if the mount point is not the root filesystem and is a local filesystem
    [[ "$mount_point" != "/" ]] && findmnt -n -o TYPE "$mount_point" | grep -q 'ext[2-4]\|xfs\|btrfs'
}

# Function to apply the necessary protections
apply_protections() {
    local mount_point=$1
    # Create a directory for device nodes if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "$mount_point/dev"
    # Mount --bind /dev to the mount point's dev directory
    mount --bind /dev "$mount_point/dev"
    # Set appropriate permissions
    chmod 755 "$mount_point/dev"
}

# Iterate over all mounts and apply protections where needed
for mount_point in $(findmnt -t ext2,ext3,ext4,xfs,btrfs -o TARGET); do
    if is_non_root_local_filesystem "$mount_point"; then
        apply_protections "$mount_point"
    fi
done

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots
```

This script checks each mount point to determine if it is a non-root local filesystem. If it is, the script creates a `dev` directory within the mount point and binds `/dev` to it. This ensures that no files can be interpreted as character or block device nodes on these partitions. The changes are also added to `/etc/fstab` to ensure they persist across reboots.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Install the SELinux policy package if not already installed
if ! rpm -q selinux-policy-targeted &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y selinux-policy-targeted
fi

# Set the SELinux mode to enforcing
setenforce 1

# Ensure the SELinux mode is set to enforcing on reboot
sed -i 's/^SELINUX=.*/SELINUX=enforcing/' /etc/selinux/config

# Create a custom SELinux module for file access policy
cat <<EOF > /tmp/file_access_policy.te
module file_access_policy 1.0;

require {
    type unconfined_t;
    type bin_t;
    class file { read getattr };
}

allow unconfined_t bin_t:file { read getattr };
EOF

# Compile the custom SELinux module
checkmodule -M -m -o /tmp/file_access_policy.mod /tmp/file_access_policy.te
semodule_package -o /tmp/file_access_policy.pp -m /tmp/file_access_policy.mod

# Load the custom SELinux module
semodule -i /tmp/file_access_policy.pp

# Clean up temporary files
rm -f /tmp/file_access_policy.te /tmp/file_access_policy.mod /tmp/file_access_policy.pp
```

update_ssh_config() {

    while IFS= read -r line; do

            echo "$key $value" >> "$temp_file"

    mv "$temp_file" "$SSH_CONFIG"
}

    echo "StrictHostKeyChecking set to 'no' successfully."

check_and_set_permissions() {
    local path=$1
    local owner=$2
    local group=$3
    local mode=$4

    # Check if the path exists
    if [ ! -e "$path" ]; then
        echo "Path $path does not exist."
        return 1
    fi

    # Set ownership and permissions
    chown ${owner}:${group} "$path"
    chmod $mode "$path"

    # Verify changes

        echo "Failed to set correct ownership and permissions for $path."
        return 1
    fi

    echo "Successfully set ownership and permissions for $path."
}

check_and_set_permissions "/var/log/secure" "root" "root" "600"
check_and_set_permissions "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" "root" "root" "600"

systemctl restart sshd

```bash
#!/bin/bash
```

# Function to generate a USBGuard policy based on current USB devices
generate_usbguard_policy() {
    # Get a list of currently connected USB devices
    local usb_devices=$(lsusb | awk -F': ' '{print $2}' | tr '\n' ',' | sed 's/,$//')

    # Create a temporary file for the new policy
    local temp_policy="/tmp/usbguard-policy.json"
    cat <<EOF > "$temp_policy"
{
    "Version": "1",
    "Rules": [
        {
            "Action": "allow",
            "Devices": ["$usb_devices"]
        }
    ]
}
EOF

    # Move the temporary policy file to its final location
    mv "$temp_policy" /etc/usbguard/rules.d/50-default-policy.json
}

# Ensure the USBGuard service is enabled and running
systemctl enable --now usbguard

# Generate and apply the USBGuard policy
generate_usbguard_policy

# Reload USBGuard to apply the new policy
usbguard reload

```bash
EOF
```

exit 0

```bash
#!/bin/bash
```

# Define the audit rule for tracking file permission changes
AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod,fchmodat -k perm_changes"
AUDIT_RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S chmod,fchmodat -k perm_changes"

# Check if the audit rule already exists
if ! auditctl -l | grep -q "$AUDIT_RULE"; then
    # Add the 64-bit audit rule
    auditctl -w /usr/bin/chmod -p x -k perm_changes
    auditctl -w /usr/bin/fchmodat -p x -k perm_changes
fi

```bash
#!/bin/bash
```

# Define the tag for grouping permission-modification events
TAG="permission_modification"

# Function to add audit rules
add_audit_rule() {
    local syscall=$1
    local arch=$2
    local user_id=$3

    # Add the audit rule
    auditctl -a exit,always -F arch=$arch -S $syscall -k $TAG -F euid>=$user_id
}

# Check if the auditd service is running
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "auditd service is not active. Please start it manually."
    exit 1
fi

# Remove existing rules related to the TAG
auditctl -D | grep "$TAG" | awk '{print $2}' | xargs -I {} auditctl -R {}

# Add audit rules for setxattr
add_audit_rule "setxattr" "b64" "1000"
add_audit_rule "lsetxattr" "b64" "1000"
add_audit_rule "fsetxattr" "b64" "1000"
add_audit_rule "setxattr" "b32" "1000"
add_audit_rule "lsetxattr" "b32" "1000"
add_audit_rule "fsetxattr" "b32" "1000"

add_audit_rule "lsetxattr" "b64" "0"
add_audit_rule "fsetxattr" "b64" "0"
add_audit_rule "setxattr" "b32" "0"
add_audit_rule "lsetxattr" "b32" "0"
add_audit_rule "fsetxattr" "b32" "0"

echo "Audit rules have been updated successfully."

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule tag
TAG="module_load_audit"

# Check if the auditd service is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &> /dev/null; then
    echo "auditd package is not installed."
    exit 1
fi

# Check if the auditd service is active
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "auditd service is not active."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the audit rules for 32-bit and 64-bit architectures
AUDIT_RULES=(
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module -k $TAG"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S init_module -k $TAG"
)

# Add the audit rules to the auditd configuration file
for RULE in "${AUDIT_RULES[@]}"; do
    auditctl -w /sbin/insmod -p x -k $TAG
    auditctl -w /sbin/modprobe -p x -k $TAG
done

# Ensure the audit rules persist across reboots
echo "Adding audit rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"
cat <<EOF | sudo tee -a /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules > /dev/null
$RULES
EOF

# Reload the audit rules
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules

echo "Audit rules added successfully."

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule tag
TAG="module_load_audit"

# Check if the auditd service is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &> /dev/null; then
    echo "auditd package is not installed."
    exit 1
fi

# Check if the auditd service is active
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "auditd service is not active."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the audit rules for 32-bit and 64-bit architectures
AUDIT_RULES=(
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module -k $TAG"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S init_module -k $TAG"
)

# Add the audit rules to the auditd configuration file
for RULE in "${AUDIT_RULES[@]}"; do
    auditctl -w /sbin/insmod -p x -k $TAG
    auditctl -w /sbin/modprobe -p x -k $TAG
done

# Ensure the audit rules persist across reboots
echo "Adding audit rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"
cat <<EOF | sudo tee -a /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules > /dev/null
$RULES
EOF

# Reload the audit rules
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules

echo "Audit rules added successfully."
```

It then defines the audit rules for both 32-bit and 64-bit architectures and adds them to the `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules` file. Finally, it reloads the audit rules to ensure they take effect immediately. The script ensures that the rules are applied even after a reboot by adding them to the audit rules file.

# Define the audit key for logins
AUDIT_KEY="logins"

# Define the paths to the system files to monitor

# Function to add audit watches
add_audit_watches() {
    local file=$1
    echo "-w $file -p wa -k $AUDIT_KEY" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
}

# Ensure the auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Add audit watches for each system file
for FILE in $SYSTEM_FILES; do
    if ! grep -q "^-w $FILE -p wa -k $AUDIT_KEY$" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules; then
        add_audit_watches "$FILE"
    fi
done

# Reload the audit rules to apply changes
augenrules --load

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the auditd service is installed
dnf install -y audit

# Configure auditd to use the FQDN as the node name
sed -i 's/^node_name = .*/node_name = $(hostname --fqdn)/' /etc/audit/auditd.conf

# Restart the auditd service to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd

# Ensure the auditd service starts on boot
systemctl enable auditd
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define the directory where audit rules are typically stored
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"

# Iterate over each file in the audit rules directory
for RULE_FILE in "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"/*.rules; do
    # Check if the file exists
    if [ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]; then
        # Change the file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write, no permissions for group/others)
        chmod 0600 "$RULE_FILE"
    fi
done
raw_response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the directory where audit rules are typically stored
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"

# Iterate over each file in the audit rules directory
for RULE_FILE in "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"/*.rules; do
    # Check if the file exists
    if [ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]; then
        # Change the file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write, no permissions for group/others)
        chmod 0600 "$RULE_FILE"
    fi
done
```
